questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A pure tungsten cathode and a thoriated-tungsten cathode share the same geometrical dimensions and are operated at a common temperature. The current produced by the thoriated-tungsten cathode is $5000$ times greater than that of the pure tungsten cathode. Determine the operating temperature. For pure tungsten, the constant $A$ in the Richardson–Dushman equation is $60 \times 10^4 \text{ A m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-2}$, while for thoriated tungsten it is $3.0 \times 10^4 \text{ A m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-2}$. Pure tungsten has a work function of $4.5 \text{ eV}$, whereas thoriated tungsten has a work function of $2.6 \text{ eV}$.
questions.solution:
Using the Richardson-Dushman equation, the emission current is given by:

$I = S A T^2 e^{-\dfrac{\Phi}{kT}}$

For pure tungsten:

$I_1 = S A_1 T^2 e^{-\dfrac{\Phi_1}{kT}}$

For thoriated tungsten:

$I_2 = S A_2 T^2 e^{-\dfrac{\Phi_2}{kT}}$

Given that $I_2 = 5000 I_1$, we get:

$S A_2 T^2 e^{-\dfrac{\Phi_2}{kT}} = 5000 S A_1 T^2 e^{-\dfrac{\Phi_1}{kT}}$

$\dfrac{A_2}{A_1} e^{\dfrac{\Phi_1 - \Phi_2}{kT}} = 5000$

Substituting the given values $A_1 = 60 \times 10^4 \text{ A m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-2}$, $A_2 = 3.0 \times 10^4 \text{ A m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-2}$, $\Phi_1 = 4.5 \text{ eV}$, and $\Phi_2 = 2.6 \text{ eV}$:

$\dfrac{3.0 \times 10^4}{60 \times 10^4} e^{\dfrac{4.5 - 2.6}{kT}} = 5000$

$\dfrac{1}{20} e^{\dfrac{1.9 \text{ eV}}{kT}} = 5000$

$e^{\dfrac{1.9 \text{ eV}}{kT}} = 100000 = 10^5$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$\dfrac{1.9 \text{ eV}}{kT} = 5 \ln 10$

Using the standard value $\dfrac{1 \text{ eV}}{k} \approx 11600 \text{ K}$ and $\ln 10 \approx 2.303$:

$T = \dfrac{1.9 \times 11600}{5 \times 2.303}$

$T = \dfrac{22040}{11.515} \approx 1914 \text{ K}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the ratio $n(T)/n(1000 \text{ K})$ for a tungsten emitter at temperatures $T = 300 \text{ K}$, $2000 \text{ K}$, and $3000 \text{ K}$. Here, $n(T)$ denotes the number of thermions emitted per second by the surface at temperature $T$. The work function of tungsten is $4.52 \text{ eV}$.
questions.solution:
According to the Richardson-Dushman equation, the number of thermions emitted per second by a surface at temperature $T$ is given by:

$n(T) \propto T^2 e^{-\dfrac{W}{k_B T}}$

The ratio of the number of thermions emitted at temperature $T$ to that at $1000 \text{ K}$ is:

$\dfrac{n(T)}{n(1000)} = \left( \dfrac{T}{1000} \right)^2 e^{-\dfrac{W}{k_B} \left( \dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{1000} \right)}$

Given the work function $W = 4.52 \text{ eV}$ and the Boltzmann constant $k_B \approx 8.62 \times 10^{-5} \text{ eV/K}$, we have:

$\dfrac{W}{k_B} = \dfrac{4.52}{8.62 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 52436 \text{ K}$

For $T = 300 \text{ K}$:

$\dfrac{n(300)}{n(1000)} = \left( \dfrac{300}{1000} \right)^2 e^{-52436 \left( \dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{1000} \right)}$

$\dfrac{n(300)}{n(1000)} = 0.09 \times e^{-122.35} \approx 0.09 \times 7.30 \times 10^{-54} = 6.57 \times 10^{-55}$

For $T = 2000 \text{ K}$:

$\dfrac{n(2000)}{n(1000)} = \left( \dfrac{2000}{1000} \right)^2 e^{-52436 \left( \dfrac{1}{2000} - \dfrac{1}{1000} \right)}$

$\dfrac{n(2000)}{n(1000)} = 4 \times e^{26.218} \approx 4 \times 2.43 \times 10^{11} = 9.73 \times 10^{11}$

For $T = 3000 \text{ K}$:

$\dfrac{n(3000)}{n(1000)} = \left( \dfrac{3000}{1000} \right)^2 e^{-52436 \left( \dfrac{1}{3000} - \dfrac{1}{1000} \right)}$

$\dfrac{n(3000)}{n(1000)} = 9 \times e^{34.957} \approx 9 \times 1.52 \times 10^{15} = 1.37 \times 10^{16}$

Answer: $6.57 \times 10^{-55}$, $9.73 \times 10^{11}$, $1.37 \times 10^{16}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Within a discharge tube, the mean free path of electrons in the gas is inversely proportional to the internal pressure. The Crookes dark space occupies half the length of the discharge tube at a pressure of $0.02 \text{ mm}$ of mercury. Estimate the pressure at which the dark space will occupy the entire tube.
questions.solution:
The length of the Crookes dark space is directly proportional to the mean free path of the electrons.

Since the mean free path is inversely proportional to the internal pressure $P$, the length of the dark space $l$ is also inversely proportional to $P$.

$l \propto \dfrac{1}{P} \Rightarrow l_1 P_1 = l_2 P_2$

Let the total length of the discharge tube be $L$.

Given that at $P_1 = 0.02 \text{ mm}$ of mercury, $l_1 = \dfrac{L}{2}$.

For the dark space to occupy the entire tube, $l_2 = L$.

Substituting the values into the equation:

$\dfrac{L}{2} \times 0.02 = L \times P_2$

$P_2 = \dfrac{0.02}{2} = 0.01 \text{ mm}$ of mercury

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $2000 \text{ K}$, the saturation current from a thoriated-tungsten cathode is $100 \text{ mA}$. Determine the saturation current for a pure-tungsten cathode that has the same surface area and operates at the same temperature. In the Richardson–Dushman equation, the constant $A$ is $60 \times 10^4 \text{ A m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-2}$ for pure tungsten and $3.0 \times 10^4 \text{ A m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-2}$ for thoriated tungsten. The work function is $4.5 \text{ eV}$ for pure tungsten, while it is $2.6 \text{ eV}$ for thoriated tungsten.
questions.solution:
According to the Richardson-Dushman equation, the saturation current is given by:

$I = S A T^2 e^{-\Phi / (kT)}$

For pure tungsten, the saturation current is:

$I_p = S A_p T^2 e^{-\Phi_p / (kT)}$

For thoriated tungsten, the saturation current is:

$I_t = S A_t T^2 e^{-\Phi_t / (kT)}$

Taking the ratio of the two currents:

$\dfrac{I_p}{I_t} = \dfrac{A_p}{A_t} e^{-(\Phi_p - \Phi_t) / (kT)}$

Substituting the given values:

$\dfrac{A_p}{A_t} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^4}{3.0 \times 10^4} = 20$

$\Phi_p - \Phi_t = 4.5 \text{ eV} - 2.6 \text{ eV} = 1.9 \text{ eV}$

$kT = \dfrac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 2000}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \text{ eV} = 0.1725 \text{ eV}$

Calculating the exponent term:

$\dfrac{\Phi_p - \Phi_t}{kT} = \dfrac{1.9}{0.1725} \approx 11.01$

$e^{-11.01} \approx 1.65 \times 10^{-5}$

Therefore, the ratio becomes:

$\dfrac{I_p}{I_t} = 20 \times 1.65 \times 10^{-5} = 3.3 \times 10^{-4}$

Since $I_t = 100 \text{ mA}$:

$I_p = 100 \text{ mA} \times 3.3 \times 10^{-4} = 33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mA} = 33 \, \mu\text{A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Within a discharge tube filled with a gas, a molecule undergoes ionization, causing an electron to be detached. An electric field of $5.0 \text{ kV m}^{-1}$ is present in the region of the event. Determine the distance travelled by the free electron in $1 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$, assuming no collisions occur.
questions.solution:
The force experienced by the electron in the electric field is given by $F = eE$.

The acceleration of the electron is $a = \dfrac{eE}{m}$.

Using the equation of motion $s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$ with initial velocity $u = 0$, the distance travelled is:

$s = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{eE}{m} \right) t^2$

Substituting the standard values $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$, $m = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$, and the given values $E = 5.0 \times 10^3 \text{ V m}^{-1}$, $t = 10^{-6} \text{ s}$:

$s = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 5.0 \times 10^3}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}} \times (10^{-6})^2$

$s = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^{-16}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}} \times 10^{-12}$

$s = \dfrac{4.0}{9.1} \times 10^3$

$s = \dfrac{4000}{9.1} \approx 439.56 \text{ m}$

Rounding to the nearest given option, the distance travelled is $440 \text{ m}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the factor by which the emission current changes when the temperature of a tungsten filament is increased from $2000 \text{ K}$ to $2010 \text{ K}$. The work function of tungsten is $4.5 \text{ eV}$.
questions.solution:
According to the Richardson-Dushman equation, the emission current $I$ is given by:

$I = A T^2 e^{-\dfrac{W}{kT}}$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$\ln I = \ln A + 2 \ln T - \dfrac{W}{kT}$

Differentiating with respect to $T$:

$\dfrac{dI}{I} = \dfrac{2}{T} dT + \dfrac{W}{kT^2} dT$

Given values are $T = 2000 \text{ K}$, $dT = 10 \text{ K}$, $W = 4.5 \text{ eV}$, and Boltzmann constant $k \approx \dfrac{1}{11600} \text{ eV/K}$. Substituting these values:

$\dfrac{dI}{I} = \dfrac{2 \times 10}{2000} + \dfrac{4.5 \times 11600 \times 10}{(2000)^2}$

$\dfrac{dI}{I} = 0.01 + \dfrac{522000}{4000000}$

$\dfrac{dI}{I} = 0.01 + 0.1305 = 0.1405$

The new emission current is $I' = I + dI = I(1 + 0.1405) = 1.1405 I$.

Thus, the emission current changes by a factor of approximately $1.14$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For tungsten, the constant $A$ in the Richardson–Dushman equation is $60 	imes 10^4 \text{ A m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-2}$, and its work function is $4.5 \text{ eV}$. An electric heater of $24 \text{ W}$ is used to heat a tungsten cathode with a surface area of $2.0 	imes 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$. In the steady state, the temperature remains constant as the heat radiated by the cathode matches the energy input from the heater. Assuming the cathode radiates as a perfect blackbody, determine the saturation current due to the emission of thermions. Use $6 	imes 10^{-8} \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-4}$ for the Stefan constant, and assume the thermions carry away a negligible fraction of the supplied heat.
questions.solution:
In the steady state, the power supplied by the heater is equal to the power radiated by the cathode as a blackbody.

$P = \sigma S T^4$

Substituting the given values:

$24 = (6 \times 10^{-8}) \times (2.0 \times 10^{-5}) \times T^4$

$24 = 12 \times 10^{-13} \times T^4$

$T^4 = \dfrac{24}{12 \times 10^{-13}} = 2 \times 10^{13} = 20 \times 10^{12} \text{ K}^4$

$T^2 = \sqrt{20} \times 10^6 \approx 4.472 \times 10^6 \text{ K}^2$

$T = \sqrt{4.472} \times 10^3 \approx 2115 \text{ K}$

Now, we calculate the exponent $\dfrac{\phi}{kT}$ for the Richardson-Dushman equation:

$\dfrac{\phi}{kT} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 2115} \approx \dfrac{7.2 \times 10^{-19}}{2.918 \times 10^{-20}} \approx 24.67$

The exponential factor is:

$e^{-\phi / kT} = e^{-24.67} \approx 1.93 \times 10^{-11}$

The saturation current $I$ is given by the Richardson-Dushman equation multiplied by the surface area:

$I = A S T^2 e^{-\phi / kT}$

Substituting the values:

$I = (60 \times 10^4) \times (2.0 \times 10^{-5}) \times (4.472 \times 10^6) \times (1.93 \times 10^{-11})$

$I = 12 \times 4.472 \times 10^6 \times 1.93 \times 10^{-11}$

$I \approx 53.66 \times 10^6 \times 1.93 \times 10^{-11} \approx 1.03 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}$

Rounding to the nearest given option, we get $I \approx 0.001 \text{ A}$.

Answer: $0.001 \text{ A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the plate voltage is $36 \text{ V}$, the power delivered in the plate circuit of a diode is $1.0 \text{ W}$. Determine the power delivered if the plate voltage is raised to $49 \text{ V}$. Assume the Langmuir–Child equation holds true.
questions.solution:
According to the Langmuir-Child equation, the plate current $I_p$ is proportional to $V_p^{3/2}$.

$I_p = k V_p^{3/2}$

The power delivered in the plate circuit is given by:

$P = V_p I_p = k V_p^{5/2}$

Given $P_1 = 1.0 \text{ W}$ at $V_{p1} = 36 \text{ V}$ and $V_{p2} = 49 \text{ V}$.

$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{V_{p2}}{V_{p1}}\right)^{5/2}$

$P_2 = 1.0 \times \left(\dfrac{49}{36}\right)^{5/2}$

$P_2 = \left(\left(\dfrac{7}{6}\right)^2\right)^{5/2} = \left(\dfrac{7}{6}\right)^5$

$P_2 = \dfrac{16807}{7776} \approx 2.16 \text{ W}$

Rounding to one decimal place, $P_2 \approx 2.2 \text{ W}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A triode valve operates with $V_p = 225 \text{ V}$ and $V_g = -0.5 \text{ V}$. If the plate voltage is raised to $250 \text{ V}$ and the grid voltage is lowered to $-2.5 \text{ V}$, the plate current is found to remain unaltered. Evaluate the amplification factor.
questions.solution:
The amplification factor $\mu$ of a triode is given by the ratio of the change in plate voltage to the change in grid voltage, keeping the plate current constant.

$\mu = - \left( \dfrac{\Delta V_p}{\Delta V_g} \right)_{\Delta I_p = 0}$

Given $V_{p1} = 225 \text{ V}$, $V_{g1} = -0.5 \text{ V}$ and $V_{p2} = 250 \text{ V}$, $V_{g2} = -2.5 \text{ V}$.

Change in plate voltage, $\Delta V_p = V_{p2} - V_{p1} = 250 - 225 = 25 \text{ V}$

Change in grid voltage, $\Delta V_g = V_{g2} - V_{g1} = -2.5 - (-0.5) = -2.0 \text{ V}$

Substituting the values:

$\mu = - \left( \dfrac{25}{-2.0} \right) = 12.5$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the amplification factor of a triode valve that has a plate resistance of $2 \text{ k}\Omega$ and a transconductance of $2 \text{ millimho}$.
questions.solution:
The amplification factor $\mu$ of a triode valve is related to its plate resistance $r_p$ and transconductance $g_m$ by the relation:

$\mu = r_p \times g_m$

Given:

$r_p = 2 \text{ k}\Omega = 2 \times 10^3 \Omega$

$g_m = 2 \text{ millimho} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \Omega^{-1}$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$\mu = (2 \times 10^3) \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) = 4$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the values of $r_p$, $\mu$, and $g_m$ for a triode operating at a plate voltage of $200 \text{ V}$ and a grid voltage of $-6 \text{ V}$. The plate characteristics are depicted in the figure.
questions.solution:
From the given plate characteristics, we identify the operating point at $V_p = 200 \text{ V}$ and $V_g = -6 \text{ V}$. At this point, the plate current is $I_p = 3 \text{ mA}$.

To find the plate resistance $r_p$, we use the formula $r_p = \left( \dfrac{\Delta V_p}{\Delta I_p} \right)_{V_g = \text{constant}}$.
Keeping $V_g = -6 \text{ V}$ constant, we select two points on this curve from the dashed box: $(V_p = 200 \text{ V}, I_p = 3 \text{ mA})$ and $(V_p = 240 \text{ V}, I_p = 8 \text{ mA})$.
$r_p = \dfrac{240 - 200}{(8 - 3) \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{40}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 8000 \text{ }\Omega = 8.0 \text{ k}\Omega$.

To find the mutual conductance $g_m$, we use the formula $g_m = \left( \dfrac{\Delta I_p}{\Delta V_g} \right)_{V_p = \text{constant}}$.
Keeping $V_p = 200 \text{ V}$ constant, we select two points on this vertical line: $(V_g = -6 \text{ V}, I_p = 3 \text{ mA})$ and $(V_g = -4 \text{ V}, I_p = 8 \text{ mA})$.
$g_m = \dfrac{(8 - 3) \times 10^{-3}}{-4 - (-6)} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A/V} = 2.5 \text{ millimho}$.

To find the amplification factor $\mu$, we use the relation $\mu = r_p \times g_m$.
$\mu = (8.0 \times 10^3) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-3}) = 20$.

Alternatively, $\mu$ can be calculated directly from the graph using $\mu = -\left( \dfrac{\Delta V_p}{\Delta V_g} \right)_{I_p = \text{constant}}$.
Keeping $I_p = 3 \text{ mA}$ constant, we use points $(V_p = 200 \text{ V}, V_g = -6 \text{ V})$ and $(V_p = 160 \text{ V}, V_g = -4 \text{ V})$.
$\mu = -\dfrac{160 - 200}{-4 - (-6)} = -\dfrac{-40}{2} = 20$.

Thus, $r_p = 8.0 \text{ k}\Omega$, $\mu = 20$, and $g_m = 2.5 \text{ millimho}$.

Answer: $8.0 \text{ k}\Omega$, $20$, and $2.5 \text{ millimho}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A triode valve has a dynamic plate resistance of $10 \text{ k}\Omega$. Determine the change in the plate current when the plate voltage is altered from $200 \text{ V}$ to $220 \text{ V}$.
questions.solution:
The dynamic plate resistance of a triode valve is given by the ratio of the change in plate voltage to the change in plate current at a constant grid voltage.

$r_p = \dfrac{\Delta V_p}{\Delta I_p}$

Given:

$r_p = 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 10 \times 10^3 \Omega$

Initial plate voltage, $V_{p1} = 200 \text{ V}$

Final plate voltage, $V_{p2} = 220 \text{ V}$

Change in plate voltage, $\Delta V_p = V_{p2} - V_{p1} = 220 - 200 = 20 \text{ V}$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$\Delta I_p = \dfrac{\Delta V_p}{r_p}$

$\Delta I_p = \dfrac{20}{10 \times 10^3}$

$\Delta I_p = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}$

$\Delta I_p = 2 \text{ mA}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A triode has a plate resistance of $8 \text{ k}\Omega$ and a transconductance of $2.5 \text{ millimho}$. Determine the increase in the plate current if the plate voltage is raised by $48 \text{ V}$ while the grid voltage is held constant.
questions.solution:
The plate resistance of a triode is defined as the ratio of change in plate voltage to the change in plate current at a constant grid voltage:

$r_p = \left( \dfrac{\Delta V_p}{\Delta I_p} \right)_{\Delta V_g = 0}$

Rearranging the formula to find the change in plate current:

$\Delta I_p = \dfrac{\Delta V_p}{r_p}$

Given that the increase in plate voltage is $\Delta V_p = 48 \text{ V}$ and the plate resistance is $r_p = 8 \text{ k}\Omega = 8 \times 10^3 \,\Omega$, substituting these values yields:

$\Delta I_p = \dfrac{48}{8 \times 10^3}$

$\Delta I_p = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} = 6 \text{ mA}$

Since the grid voltage is held constant, the transconductance ($g_m$) is not required for this calculation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A triode has a plate resistance of $8 \text{ k}\Omega$ and a transconductance of $2.5 \text{ millimho}$. The plate voltage is increased by $48 \text{ V}$. If the plate voltage is held constant at this new elevated value, by how much must the grid voltage be reduced to restore the plate current to its initial value?
questions.solution:
Given:
Plate resistance $r_p = 8 \text{ k}\Omega = 8 \times 10^3 \Omega$
Transconductance $g_m = 2.5 \text{ millimho} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mho}$
Change in plate voltage $\Delta V_p = 48 \text{ V}$

The amplification factor $\mu$ is given by the product of plate resistance and transconductance:
$\mu = r_p \times g_m$
$\mu = (8 \times 10^3) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-3}) = 20$

For a constant plate current ($\Delta I_p = 0$), the relation between the change in plate voltage and the change in grid voltage is:
$\mu = -\dfrac{\Delta V_p}{\Delta V_g}$

Substituting the values:
$20 = -\dfrac{48}{\Delta V_g}$
$\Delta V_g = -\dfrac{48}{20} = -2.4 \text{ V}$

The negative sign indicates that the grid voltage must be reduced. The required reduction is $2.4 \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A triode has a plate resistance of $10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and an amplification factor of $20$. It is operated at a plate voltage of $250 \text{ V}$ and a grid voltage of $-7.5 \text{ V}$, resulting in a plate current of $10 \text{ mA}$. To what value must the grid voltage be adjusted in order to increase the plate current to $15 \text{ mA}$?
questions.solution:
Given:
Plate resistance, $r_p = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$
Amplification factor, $\mu = 20$
Initial plate current, $I_{p1} = 10 \text{ mA}$
Final plate current, $I_{p2} = 15 \text{ mA}$
Initial grid voltage, $V_{g1} = -7.5 \text{ V}$

The mutual conductance $g_m$ is given by:
$g_m = \dfrac{\mu}{r_p} = \dfrac{20}{10 \text{ k}\Omega} = 2 \text{ mA/V}$

The change in plate current is:
$\Delta I_p = I_{p2} - I_{p1} = 15 \text{ mA} - 10 \text{ mA} = 5 \text{ mA}$

For a constant plate voltage ($\Delta V_p = 0$), the change in plate current is related to the change in grid voltage by:
$\Delta I_p = g_m \Delta V_g$

Substituting the values:
$5 \text{ mA} = 2 \text{ mA/V} \times \Delta V_g$
$\Delta V_g = 2.5 \text{ V}$

The new grid voltage $V_{g2}$ is:
$V_{g2} = V_{g1} + \Delta V_g = -7.5 \text{ V} + 2.5 \text{ V} = -5.0 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A triode has a plate resistance of $10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and an amplification factor of $20$. Initially, it is operated at a plate voltage of $250 \text{ V}$ and a grid voltage of $-7.5 \text{ V}$, yielding a plate current of $10 \text{ mA}$. The grid voltage is subsequently adjusted to $-5.0 \text{ V}$, increasing the plate current to $15 \text{ mA}$. To what value must the plate voltage be altered to bring the plate current back to $10 \text{ mA}$?
questions.solution:
For a triode, the change in plate current $\Delta I_p$ is given by:

$\Delta I_p = \dfrac{\Delta V_p}{r_p} + g_m \Delta V_g$

where $g_m = \dfrac{\mu}{r_p}$.

To bring the plate current back to its initial value of $10 \text{ mA}$, the net change in plate current from the initial state must be zero ($\Delta I_p = 0$).

Substituting $\Delta I_p = 0$ into the equation:

$0 = \dfrac{\Delta V_p}{r_p} + \dfrac{\mu}{r_p} \Delta V_g$

$\Delta V_p = -\mu \Delta V_g$

The change in grid voltage from the initial state is:

$\Delta V_g = -5.0 - (-7.5) = 2.5 \text{ V}$

Using the given amplification factor $\mu = 20$:

$\Delta V_p = -20 \times 2.5 = -50 \text{ V}$

The new plate voltage is:

$V_p = 250 - 50 = 200 \text{ V}$

Alternatively, from the intermediate state ($I_p = 15 \text{ mA}$, $V_p = 250 \text{ V}$), to reduce the current to $10 \text{ mA}$ ($\Delta I_p = -5 \text{ mA}$) at a constant grid voltage:

$\Delta V_p = \Delta I_p \times r_p = (-5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (10 \times 10^3) = -50 \text{ V}$

The required plate voltage is $250 - 50 = 200 \text{ V}$.

Answer: $200 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Within a discharge tube filled with a gas, a molecule undergoes ionization, causing an electron to be detached. An electric field of $5.0 \text{ kV m}^{-1}$ is present in the region of the event. Given that the mean free path of the electron is $1.0 \text{ mm}$, estimate the transit time of the free electron between successive collisions.
questions.solution:
The acceleration of the electron in the electric field is given by:

$a = \dfrac{eE}{m}$

Assuming the electron starts from rest after being detached, the distance traveled between successive collisions is the mean free path $\lambda$. Using the equation of motion $s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$ with $u = 0$ and $s = \lambda$:

$\lambda = \dfrac{1}{2}at^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{eE}{m} \right) t^2$

Rearranging for the transit time $t$:

$t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2m\lambda}{eE}}$

Substituting the given values ($E = 5.0 \times 10^3 \text{ V m}^{-1}$, $\lambda = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $m = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$, $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$):

$t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 5.0 \times 10^3}}$

$t = \sqrt{\dfrac{18.2 \times 10^{-34}}{8.0 \times 10^{-16}}}$

$t = \sqrt{2.275 \times 10^{-18}}$

$t \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 1.5 \text{ ns}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Helium at a low pressure is contained within a discharge tube. A large potential difference is applied across this tube. Consider a helium atom that has just been ionized by the detachment of an atomic electron. Determine the ratio of the distance travelled by the free electron to that by the positive ion in a short time interval $dt$ following the ionization.
questions.solution:
Let $E$ be the electric field applied across the discharge tube.

The force on the free electron is $F_e = eE$, and its acceleration is $a_e = \dfrac{eE}{m_e}$.

Since the helium atom is ionized by the detachment of a single electron, the charge on the helium ion (He$^+$) is $+e$.

The force on the helium ion is $F_i = eE$, and its acceleration is $a_i = \dfrac{eE}{m_i}$.

The distance travelled by a particle starting from rest in a short time interval $dt$ is given by $s = \dfrac{1}{2}a(dt)^2$.

The ratio of the distances travelled by the electron and the helium ion is:

$\dfrac{s_e}{s_i} = \dfrac{a_e}{a_i} = \dfrac{m_i}{m_e}$

The mass of a helium ion is approximately $4$ times the mass of a proton ($m_p$).

$m_i \approx 4m_p$

Using the standard ratio $\dfrac{m_p}{m_e} \approx 1836$, we get:

$\dfrac{s_e}{s_i} \approx 4 \times 1836 = 7344$

The closest value among the given options is $7340$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two discharge tubes possess identical material structure and are filled with the same gas. The length of one tube is $10 \text{ cm}$ while that of the second tube is $20 \text{ cm}$. Sparking initiates in both tubes when the potential difference between the cathode and the anode reaches $100 \text{ V}$. If the pressure in the shorter tube is $1.0 \text{ mm}$ of mercury, determine the pressure in the longer tube.
questions.solution:
According to Paschen's law, the sparking potential $V$ of a gas between two electrodes depends only on the product of the gas pressure $p$ and the distance $d$ between the electrodes.

$V = f(pd)$

Since the sparking potential is the same for both tubes, the product of pressure and length must be constant.

$p_1 d_1 = p_2 d_2$

Substituting the given values $p_1 = 1.0 \text{ mm}$ of Hg, $d_1 = 10 \text{ cm}$, and $d_2 = 20 \text{ cm}$:

$1.0 \times 10 = p_2 \times 20$

$p_2 = \dfrac{10}{20} = 0.5 \text{ mm}$ of Hg

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a 6F6 triode tube, the plate current, plate voltage, and grid voltage are related by $i_p = 41(V_p + 7 V_g)^{1.41}$, where $V_p$ and $V_g$ are in volts and $i_p$ is in microamperes. If the tube is operated at $V_p = 250 \text{ V}$ and $V_g = -20 \text{ V}$, calculate the tube current.
questions.solution:
Given the equation for the plate current:

$i_p = 41(V_p + 7 V_g)^{1.41}$

Substitute the given values $V_p = 250 \text{ V}$ and $V_g = -20 \text{ V}$ into the equation:

$i_p = 41(250 + 7(-20))^{1.41}$

$i_p = 41(250 - 140)^{1.41}$

$i_p = 41(110)^{1.41}$

Using logarithms or a calculator to evaluate $(110)^{1.41}$:

$\log_{10}(110^{1.41}) = 1.41 \times \log_{10}(110) \approx 1.41 \times 2.0414 \approx 2.878$

$110^{1.41} \approx 10^{2.878} \approx 755.7$

Now, substituting this back into the equation for $i_p$:

$i_p \approx 41 \times 755.7 \text{ } \mu\text{A}$

$i_p \approx 30984 \text{ } \mu\text{A}$

Converting microamperes to milliamperes ($1 \text{ mA} = 1000 \text{ } \mu\text{A}$):

$i_p \approx 30.98 \text{ mA}$

Rounding to the nearest given option, we get $30 \text{ mA}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a 6F6 triode tube, the plate current, plate voltage, and grid voltage are related by $i_p = 41(V_p + 7 V_g)^{1.41}$, where $V_p$ and $V_g$ are in volts and $i_p$ is in microamperes. If the tube is operated at $V_p = 250 \text{ V}$ and $V_g = -20 \text{ V}$, calculate the mutual conductance.
questions.solution:
The mutual conductance $g_m$ of a triode is defined as the rate of change of plate current with respect to grid voltage, keeping the plate voltage constant.

$g_m = \left( \dfrac{\partial i_p}{\partial V_g} \right)_{V_p}$

Given the relation for the plate current:

$i_p = 41(V_p + 7 V_g)^{1.41}$

Differentiating $i_p$ with respect to $V_g$:

$g_m = 41 \times 1.41 \times (V_p + 7 V_g)^{1.41 - 1} \times 7$

$g_m = 404.67 (V_p + 7 V_g)^{0.41}$

Substituting $V_p = 250 \text{ V}$ and $V_g = -20 \text{ V}$:

$g_m = 404.67 (250 + 7(-20))^{0.41}$

$g_m = 404.67 (250 - 140)^{0.41}$

$g_m = 404.67 (110)^{0.41}$

$g_m \approx 404.67 \times 6.87$

$g_m \approx 2780 \text{ } \mu\text{mho}$

$g_m \approx 2.78 \text{ millimho}$

The closest given option is $2.77 \text{ millimho}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The plate current in a triode can be written as $i_p = k\!\left(V_g + \dfrac{V_p}{\mu}\right)^{3/2}$. The mutual conductance is proportional to what power of the plate current?
questions.solution:
The plate current in a triode is given by:

$i_p = k \left(V_g + \dfrac{V_p}{\mu}\right)^{3/2}$

The mutual conductance $g_m$ is defined as the rate of change of plate current with respect to grid voltage at a constant plate voltage:

$g_m = \dfrac{\partial i_p}{\partial V_g}$

Differentiating the given expression with respect to $V_g$:

$g_m = \dfrac{3}{2} k \left(V_g + \dfrac{V_p}{\mu}\right)^{1/2}$

From the equation for plate current, we can write:

$\left(V_g + \dfrac{V_p}{\mu}\right)^{3/2} = \dfrac{i_p}{k}$

$\left(V_g + \dfrac{V_p}{\mu}\right)^{1/2} = \left(\dfrac{i_p}{k}\right)^{1/3}$

Substituting this back into the expression for mutual conductance:

$g_m = \dfrac{3}{2} k \left(\dfrac{i_p}{k}\right)^{1/3}$

$g_m = \dfrac{3}{2} k^{2/3} i_p^{1/3}$

Thus, $g_m \propto i_p^{1/3}$

The mutual conductance is proportional to the $1/3$ power of the plate current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A triode possesses a mutual conductance of $2.0 \text{ millimho}$ and a plate resistance of $20 \text{ k}\Omega$. To amplify a signal by a factor of $30$, what load resistance must be incorporated into the circuit?
questions.solution:
Given $g_m = 2.0 \text{ millimho} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mho}$ and $r_p = 20 \text{ k}\Omega = 20 \times 10^3 \Omega$.

The amplification factor $\mu$ is given by:

$\mu = r_p \times g_m$

$\mu = (20 \times 10^3) \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) = 40$

The voltage gain $A_v$ of the amplifier is given by:

$A_v = \dfrac{\mu R_L}{r_p + R_L}$

Substituting the given values:

$30 = \dfrac{40 R_L}{20 + R_L}$

$600 + 30 R_L = 40 R_L$

$10 R_L = 600$

$R_L = 60 \text{ k}\Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the load resistance of an amplifier is modified from $4 \text{ k}\Omega$ to $8 \text{ k}\Omega$, its gain factor increases from $10$ to $12$. Determine the amplification factor.
questions.solution:
The voltage gain $A_v$ of an amplifier is given by the relation:

$A_v = \dfrac{\mu R_L}{r_p + R_L}$

where $\mu$ is the amplification factor, $R_L$ is the load resistance, and $r_p$ is the internal resistance.

For the first case, $R_L = 4 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $A_v = 10$:

$10 = \dfrac{4\mu}{r_p + 4}$

$10r_p + 40 = 4\mu \Rightarrow 5r_p + 20 = 2\mu$

For the second case, $R_L = 8 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $A_v = 12$:

$12 = \dfrac{8\mu}{r_p + 8}$

$12r_p + 96 = 8\mu \Rightarrow 3r_p + 24 = 2\mu$

Equating the expressions for $2\mu$:

$5r_p + 20 = 3r_p + 24$

$2r_p = 4 \Rightarrow r_p = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$

Substituting the value of $r_p$ into the equation for $2\mu$:

$2\mu = 5(2) + 20 = 30$

$\mu = 15$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the load resistance of an amplifier is modified from $4 \text{ k}\Omega$ to $8 \text{ k}\Omega$, its gain factor increases from $10$ to $12$. Determine the plate resistance.
questions.solution:
The voltage gain of an amplifier is given by $A = \dfrac{\mu R_L}{R_p + R_L}$

where $\mu$ is the amplification factor, $R_p$ is the plate resistance, and $R_L$ is the load resistance.

For the first case:

$10 = \dfrac{\mu \times 4}{R_p + 4}$

For the second case:

$12 = \dfrac{\mu \times 8}{R_p + 8}$

Dividing the first equation by the second equation:

$\dfrac{10}{12} = \dfrac{4(R_p + 8)}{8(R_p + 4)}$

$\dfrac{5}{6} = \dfrac{R_p + 8}{2(R_p + 4)}$

$10(R_p + 4) = 6(R_p + 8)$

$10R_p + 40 = 6R_p + 48$

$4R_p = 8$

$R_p = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a diode, the plate current is $20 \text{ mA}$ when the plate voltage is $50 \text{ V}$ or $60 \text{ V}$. What will be the current if the plate voltage is $70 \text{ V}$?
questions.solution:
Since the plate current is constant at $20 \text{ mA}$ for plate voltages of $50 \text{ V}$ and $60 \text{ V}$, the diode has reached its saturation state.

In the saturation region, the plate current becomes independent of the plate voltage and depends only on the temperature of the cathode.

Therefore, when the plate voltage is increased to $70 \text{ V}$, the plate current will remain at the saturation value of $20 \text{ mA}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical triode tubes are connected in parallel, as shown in the figure. The anodes are joined together, the grids are joined together, and the cathodes are joined together. Assuming each tube possesses a plate resistance $r_p$, a mutual conductance $g_m$, and an amplification factor $\mu$, find the equivalent plate resistance, equivalent mutual conductance, and equivalent amplification factor of the parallel combination.
questions.solution:
For a single triode, the small change in plate current $\Delta i_p$ is given by:

$\Delta i_p = g_m \Delta v_g + \dfrac{1}{r_p} \Delta v_p$

When two identical triodes are connected in parallel, their grids, plates, and cathodes are joined together. Therefore, they share the same grid voltage change $\Delta v_g$ and plate voltage change $\Delta v_p$.

The total plate current change $\Delta i_{p,eq}$ is the sum of the individual plate current changes:

$\Delta i_{p,eq} = \Delta i_{p1} + \Delta i_{p2}$

Since the triodes are identical, substituting the expressions gives:

$\Delta i_{p,eq} = 2 \left( g_m \Delta v_g + \dfrac{1}{r_p} \Delta v_p \right)$

$\Delta i_{p,eq} = (2g_m) \Delta v_g + \left( \dfrac{2}{r_p} \right) \Delta v_p$

For the equivalent triode, the equation is:

$\Delta i_{p,eq} = g_{m,eq} \Delta v_g + \dfrac{1}{r_{p,eq}} \Delta v_p$

Comparing the coefficients, we obtain:

$g_{m,eq} = 2g_m$

$\dfrac{1}{r_{p,eq}} = \dfrac{2}{r_p} \Rightarrow r_{p,eq} = \dfrac{r_p}{2}$

The equivalent amplification factor $\mu_{eq}$ is given by the product of equivalent mutual conductance and equivalent plate resistance:

$\mu_{eq} = g_{m,eq} \times r_{p,eq}$

$\mu_{eq} = (2g_m) \times \left( \dfrac{r_p}{2} \right) = g_m r_p$

Since $\mu = g_m r_p$ for a single triode, we have:

$\mu_{eq} = \mu$

Thus, the equivalent parameters are $\dfrac{r_p}{2}$, $2g_m$, and $\mu$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When $60 \text{ volts}$ are applied across a diode tube, a plate current of $10 \text{ mA}$ is obtained. Assuming that the Langmuir–Child equation $i_p \propto V_p^{3/2}$ is valid, determine the dynamic resistance $r_p$ in this operating condition.
questions.solution:
According to the Langmuir-Child equation, the plate current is given by:

$i_p = k V_p^{3/2}$

Differentiating with respect to $V_p$, we get:

$\dfrac{di_p}{dV_p} = \dfrac{3}{2} k V_p^{1/2}$

Multiplying and dividing by $V_p$ gives:

$\dfrac{di_p}{dV_p} = \dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{k V_p^{3/2}}{V_p} = \dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{i_p}{V_p}$

The dynamic resistance $r_p$ is the reciprocal of this derivative:

$r_p = \dfrac{dV_p}{di_p} = \dfrac{2}{3} \dfrac{V_p}{i_p}$

Substituting the given values $V_p = 60 \text{ V}$ and $i_p = 10 \text{ mA} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}$:

$r_p = \dfrac{2}{3} \times \dfrac{60}{10 \times 10^{-3}}$

$r_p = \dfrac{2}{3} \times 6000 = 4000 \text{ }\Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a 6F6 triode tube, the plate current, plate voltage, and grid voltage are related by $i_p = 41(V_p + 7 V_g)^{1.41}$, where $V_p$ and $V_g$ are in volts and $i_p$ is in microamperes. If the tube is operated at $V_p = 250 \text{ V}$ and $V_g = -20 \text{ V}$, calculate the plate resistance.
questions.solution:
The plate current is given by:

$i_p = 41(V_p + 7 V_g)^{1.41} \text{ \mu A}$

The plate resistance $r_p$ is defined as the reciprocal of the partial derivative of plate current with respect to plate voltage at a constant grid voltage:

$r_p = \left( \dfrac{\partial V_p}{\partial i_p} \right)_{V_g} = \dfrac{1}{\left( \dfrac{\partial i_p}{\partial V_p} \right)_{V_g}}$

Differentiating $i_p$ with respect to $V_p$ keeping $V_g$ constant:

$\dfrac{\partial i_p}{\partial V_p} = 41 \times 1.41 (V_p + 7 V_g)^{1.41 - 1} \text{ \mu A/V}$

$\dfrac{\partial i_p}{\partial V_p} = 57.81 (V_p + 7 V_g)^{0.41} \text{ \mu A/V}$

Substitute the given operating values $V_p = 250 \text{ V}$ and $V_g = -20 \text{ V}$:

$V_p + 7 V_g = 250 + 7(-20) = 250 - 140 = 110 \text{ V}$

Now, calculate the derivative:

$\dfrac{\partial i_p}{\partial V_p} = 57.81 \times (110)^{0.41} \text{ \mu A/V}$

Using $(110)^{0.41} \approx 6.87$:

$\dfrac{\partial i_p}{\partial V_p} \approx 57.81 \times 6.87 \approx 397.15 \text{ \mu A/V} = 397.15 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/V}$

The plate resistance is:

$r_p = \dfrac{1}{397.15 \times 10^{-6}} \text{ }\Omega \approx 2518 \text{ }\Omega \approx 2.52 \text{ k}\Omega$

The closest matching option is $2.53 \text{ k}\Omega$.

Answer: $2.53 \text{ k}\Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a 6F6 triode tube, the plate current, plate voltage, and grid voltage are related by $i_p = 41(V_p + 7 V_g)^{1.41}$, where $V_p$ and $V_g$ are in volts and $i_p$ is in microamperes. If the tube is operated at $V_p = 250 \text{ V}$ and $V_g = -20 \text{ V}$, calculate the amplification factor.
questions.solution:
The plate current of a triode is generally expressed as $i_p = K(V_p + \mu V_g)^n$, where $\mu$ is the amplification factor.

Comparing the given equation $i_p = 41(V_p + 7 V_g)^{1.41}$ with the standard equation, we directly get $\mu = 7$.

Alternatively, the amplification factor is defined as $\mu = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V_p}{\partial V_g}\right)_{i_p}$.

For a constant $i_p$, differentiating the given equation yields:

$0 = 41 \times 1.41 (V_p + 7 V_g)^{0.41} (dV_p + 7 dV_g)$

$dV_p + 7 dV_g = 0$

$\mu = -\dfrac{dV_p}{dV_g} = 7$
